examples.title: RIP Route Update Algorithm
examples.description: Demonstrates how RIP processes incoming routing updates using Bellman-Ford principles
examples.code:
// RIP Route Update Process
PROCEDURE ProcessRIPUpdate(receivedRoute, neighborAddress):
 FOR EACH route IN receivedRoute:
 // Add 1 hop for the neighbor
 newMetric = route.metric + 1
 
 // Check if metric exceeds infinity
 IF newMetric > 16 THEN
 newMetric = 16
 END IF
 
 // Check if we already have this route
 existingRoute = LookupRouteTable(route.destination)
 
 IF existingRoute = NULL THEN
 // New destination discovered
 AddToRouteTable(route.destination, newMetric, neighborAddress)
 ELSE IF neighborAddress = existingRoute.nextHop THEN
 // Same neighbor, always update
 IF newMetric != existingRoute.metric THEN
 existingRoute.metric = newMetric
 SetTimer(existingRoute, invalidTimer)
 END IF
 ELSE IF newMetric < existingRoute.metric THEN
 // Better route found
 existingRoute.metric = newMetric
 existingRoute.nextHop = neighborAddress
 SetTimer(existingRoute, invalidTimer)
 END IF
 END FOR
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows how RIP processes incoming routing updates. It adds 1 to received metrics (Bellman-Ford), compares with existing routes, and updates only if the new route is better. Routes with metric 16 are unreachable.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of routes in update
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m) where m is size of routing table

examples.title: RIP Periodic Update Timer
examples.description: Shows how RIP generates and sends periodic updates every 30 seconds
examples.code:
// RIP Periodic Update Generation
PROCEDURE RIPPeriodicUpdate():
 // Triggered by Update Timer (expires every 30 seconds)
 
 FOR EACH interface IN router.interfaces:
 // Get all routes in routing table
 routesToSend = GetAllRoutes()
 
 // Apply split horizon (don't send routes learned from this interface)
 FOR EACH route IN routesToSend:
 IF route.interface != interface THEN
 AddToUpdateMessage(route)
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Send update via appropriate method
 IF RIPVersion = 1 THEN
 SendBroadcast(interface, updateMessage) // 255.255.255.255
 ELSE
 SendMulticast(interface, 224.0.0.9, updateMessage) // RIPv2
 END IF
 END FOR
 
 // Reset the update timer
 ResetTimer(updateTimer, 30 seconds)
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Every 30 seconds, RIP generates routing updates for all interfaces. RIPv1 uses broadcast, RIPv2 uses multicast. Split horizon is applied to prevent routing loops.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is routing table size

examples.title: RIP Holddown Timer Logic
examples.description: Demonstrates how holddown prevents accepting potentially bad route updates
examples.code:
// RIP Holddown Logic
PROCEDURE OnRouteChange(destination, newMetric, fromNeighbor):
 currentRoute = LookupRouteTable(destination)
 
 // If route was not in table or is worse
 IF currentRoute = NULL THEN
 AddRoute(destination, newMetric, fromNeighbor)
 StartHolddown(destination, fromNeighbor, 180 seconds)
 ELSE IF fromNeighbor = currentRoute.nextHop THEN
 // Same neighbor - update immediately
 currentRoute.metric = newMetric
 ResetTimer(currentRoute)
 
 // If now unreachable, start holddown
 IF newMetric = 16 THEN
 StartHolddown(destination, fromNeighbor, 180 seconds)
 END IF
 ELSE IF newMetric < currentRoute.metric THEN
 // Check if holddown is active
 IF IsHolddownActive(destination) THEN
 // Ignore better route during holddown
 Log("Route to", destination, "in holddown")
 ELSE
 // Accept better route
 currentRoute.metric = newMetric
 currentRoute.nextHop = fromNeighbor
 ResetTimer(currentRoute)
 END IF
 END IF
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: Holddown prevents accepting potentially false route updates. After a route becomes unreachable, the router ignores any updates about that destination for 180 seconds, allowing time for consistent information to propagate.

examples.title: Route Poisoning Implementation
examples.description: Shows how route poisoning advertises unreachable routes with metric 16
examples.explanation: When a network fails, RIP immediately advertises it with metric 16 (poisoning) to all neighbors. This is faster than waiting for neighbors to timeout. Poison reverse ensures the bad news spreads quickly.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is number of interfaces
